attributes.stem:
An 82-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with gradual onset breathlessness over the past day. He has severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (GOLD stage 4) and is known to the palliative care service. His medical history includes ischaemic heart disease, osteoarthritis, and anxiety. His medications include tiotropium inhaler, fluticasone/vilanterol inhaler, salbutamol inhaler, aspirin, and ambulatory home oxygen therapy (2 L/min). He lives at home with his wife and remains independent with activities of daily living.

On examination, he appears mildly short of breath, but can speak full sentences. His pulse rate is 92/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 91% on 2 L/min oxygen, and temperature 36.5°C. There is reduced air entry and hyperresonance to percussion on the right side.

A chest X-ray reveals a small right-sided pneumothorax.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents an elderly man with advanced chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) who has developed a secondary pneumothorax. The management decision needs to consider his underlying disease severity, functional status, and goals of care. There is evidence of increased mortality of elderly patients with COPD who develop a spontaneous pneumothorax, and given this patients background an emphasis on comfort based care is the best approach.

Conservative management with oxygen therapy is most appropriate given the small size of the pneumothorax, his stable clinical condition, and advanced underlying disease. Supplemental oxygen can help with both pneumothorax resorption and symptom management. This approach aligns with palliative principles of minimising invasive interventions when risks outweigh benefits.

Needle aspiration is an invasive procedure that carries risks of pain, bleeding, and infection. Given this patient's advanced disease and stable condition, the risks of this intervention outweigh potential benefits.

Chest drain insertion would be overly aggressive in this context. While it might be appropriate for larger pneumothoraces or unstable patients, it would cause unnecessary discomfort and potential complications in this situation.

Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery would be an inappropriately aggressive intervention given the patient's advanced disease state and the small size of the pneumothorax. The surgical risks would far outweigh any potential benefits.

Pleurodesis is an invasive procedure typically reserved for recurrent pneumothoraces in patients with better functional status and longer life expectancy. It would not be appropriate given this patient's advanced disease state and the first presentation of a small pneumothorax.
learningPoints: Conservative management is appropriate for small secondary pneumothoraces in patients with advanced disease where risks of intervention outweigh benefits.
Management decisions in secondary pneumothorax should consider patient's goals of care, functional status, and disease trajectory.
Oxygen therapy can help resorption of pneumothorax while providing symptomatic relief.
references: Melhorn J, Davies HE. The management of subcutaneous emphysema in pneumothorax: a literature review. Current Pulmonology Reports. 2021 Jun;10:92-7. Available online at ; https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/nejmoa1910775

Nishizawa S, Tobino K, Murakami Y, Uchida K, Kawabata T, Ota H, Hiramatsu Y, Sueyasu T, Tsuruno K. Mortality and prognostic factors for spontaneous pneumothorax in older adults. Plos one. 2023 Sep 8;18(9):e0291233. Available online at ; https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0291233
